title-group.article-title: Scar Quality After Burns in Relation to Skin Type, Classified by Device‐Based Colour Measurement, as an Alternative for the Fitzpatrick Questionnaire

aff.named-content: Alliance of Dutch Burn Care, Burn Centre
Amsterdam UMC Location Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam
aff.institution: Department of Plastic, Reconstructive and Hand Surgery
Tissue Function and Regeneration, Amsterdam UMC

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). International Wound Journal published by Medicalhelplines.com Inc and John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: This study aimed to determine if skin types can be accurately assessed via melanin scores measured with a colormeter, and whether scar characteristics of people with different skin types are different at various stages of healing. Melanin scores of a Cortex DSM Colormeter were validated against the Fitzpatrick skin type questionnaire in healthy volunteers and unaffected skin of burn and scar patients (N = 137) to determine melanin index cut‐offs per skin type. Scar quality in children and adults at 3, 6, and 12 months post‐burn using DSM Colormeter and the Patient and Observer Scar Assessment Scale (POSAS) was analysed in a cohort of > 1000 patients. Data from 137 individuals showed good correlation between melanin index and Fitzpatrick skin types (Spearman's rho: 0.72 Dermaspectrometer, 0.69 DSM II; p < 0.0001). Scar characteristics varied by skin type for erythema, pigmentation, vascularization, and overall POSAS scores. Children had significantly thicker scars than adults, regardless of skin type, varying from 0.3 to 1.4 points difference on the observer scale at 12 months post burn. We conclude that skin type can be objectively classified using the melanin index, showing a strong correlation with the Fitzpatrick questionnaire. Skin type influences scar outcomes, especially pigmentation and vascularization. Children, particularly those with lighter skin, tend to develop thicker scars than adults. Recognising these differences can improve scar management and patient education.

p.mixed-citation: K. L. M.Gardien, A.Pijpe, K.Kwa, M. B. A.van der Wal, W. E.Tuinebreijer, and E.Middelkoop, “Scar Quality After Burns in Relation to Skin Type, Classified by Device‐Based Colour Measurement, as an Alternative for the Fitzpatrick Questionnaire,” International Wound Journal22, no. 11 (2025): e70788, 10.1111/iwj.70788.

fn.p: Funding: The authors received no specific funding for this work.

list-item.p: The Fitzpatrick questionnaire is frequently used to assess a person's skin type, but this tool was originally designed to determine photosensitivity of the skin. For assessment of skin type in relation to sensitivity to scar formation, this tool is less suitable.
Colormeter assessment of healthy skin showed good correlation to the Fitzpatrick questionnaire, and is an easy to use tool.
Scar quality, especially vascularisation and pigmentation, is influenced by a person's skin type.
Children, especially children with lighter skin, tend to develop thicker scars than adults.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Skin typing is considered important in many different fields, such as determining photosensitivity before laser treatment, UV therapy, radiation in oncological treatment, but also before skin rejuvenation treatments such as chemical peelings [1, 2, 3, 4]. Furthermore, skin type is of influence on individual characteristics such as risk of skin cancer or predisposition to scar formation, which is most likely related to genetic differences [5]. Valid, reliable and feasible methods to assess a person's skin type, however, are scarce. Since many years, the Fitzpatrick questionnaire is used to categorise an individual's skin type [6, 7]. This questionnaire is based on scoring three main categories using self‐reporting on different aspects of the following: genetic disposition (e.g., eye colour), reaction to sun exposure and tanning habits. The sum score determines the skin type category, which consists of six different categories (I–VI), varying from a lighter to darker skin tone [8]. Nevertheless, there are several disadvantages to the use of the Fitzpatrick questionnaire. The self‐reportage of patients may vary during the season (e.g., tanning occurs more during the summer), and may lead to response bias. For instance, patients are questioned regarding their hair colour, which may vary as patients sometimes fail to report their natural hair colour [9]. Furthermore, the result of this scale is sensitive to exact wording as changing the wording of a question was shown to influence the classification into skin phototype [10]. Sharma et al. [11] adapted the questionnaire for a specific population and in a specific group of individuals only the sun exposure part of the scale was found to have a good internal consistency and construct validity [12]. Alternative skin tone scales such as the Monk skin tone scale might provide easier and reliable methods for assessment of skin type (see Weir et al. for a review) [13], although practical disadvantages, eg due to colour differences related to printing, also have been reported [14].

Fitzpatrick skin type (FST) was developed to assess the propensity of the skin to burn during phototherapy [15, 16], but has been used in many other fields since [1, 4]. For phototherapy, alternative methods have been suggested by determining minimal erythema dose (MED) or melanogenesis dose (MMD) [17]. However, for other application areas, this method might not be suitable. As already suggested in one of the early publications of the Fitzpatrick classification, objective skin colour measurement might provide a good alternative for skin type assessment [6].

The measurement of skin colour as well as scar colour can be objectively performed with different measurement techniques. One measurement instrument is the Dermaspectrometer or its successors DSM II–IV ColorMeter (Cortex, Hadsund, Denmark) which is a narrow‐band reflectance spectrophotometer that measures an erythema and melanin index, respectively representing vascularization and pigmentation [18]. The measurement of scar colour using the DSM ColorMeter showed reliable results with a single measurement by one observer, and was preferred to other scar colour measurement devices [19, 20].

Genetic differences give rise to differences in scar colour and pigmentation but also may predispose individuals to more profound scar pathology such as hypertrophic scars and keloids [5, 21, 22] resulting in more obvious differences between the scar and healthy skin within a patient. Burn injuries frequently give rise to scar formation. This may affect the quality of life of a burn patient [23, 24]. Scar quality can be assessed by various scar characteristics, which often include visual (e.g., colour) aspects. The assessment of scar quality is important to monitor patient recovery, evaluate treatment effects, and innovate treatment modalities. There are several subjective and objective scar assessment tools available to measure these characteristics [25, 26].

One of the most frequently used scar assessment scales is the patient and observer scar assessment scale (POSAS) [27], which consists of an observer as well as a patient scale [28, 29, 30, 31, 32]. The importance of skin colour in the development and validation of scar assessment tools as well as in studying treatment outcomes is gaining more attention in recent years [33].

Some evidence exists in the literature linking skin type to the extent of specific aspects of scar formation [5, 34]. This generally indicates that persons with darker skin tones are at a higher risk for pronounced hypertrophic scarring. The association between lighter skin categories and the quality of scarring is less studied, but relevant in view of patient information and the necessity to take (more) preventive measures during treatment.

Based on the limitations of the Fitzpatrick skin type questionnaire and the lack of data on the association between all skin types and scar formation, the aim of our study was twofold: to determine if colour measurement can be used to assess skin type, and if different skin types lead to differences in scar quality after burns. First, in order to classify patients into different skin types, we created a categorical system based on melanin scores. This was done by validating melanin scores, using narrow‐band reflectance spectrophotometry, against the Fitzpatrick skin type questionnaire classification in healthy volunteers and in scar and burn patients, in whom the unaffected skin was measured. Second, we assessed burn scar quality in > 1000 patients during the first year post‐burn, as determined by the POSAS, in relation to their unaffected skin melanin score based skin type.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This observational study made use of data collected in a cross‐sectional study (skin database), and prospectively collected data from a real‐world clinical setting (scar database).

sec.title: Skin Database
sec.p: The skin database was composed of in total 137 healthy volunteers and patients recruited at the burn centre of the Red Cross Hospital in Beverwijk and Amsterdam UMC (location VU Medical Centre) in Amsterdam. Individuals first filled in the Fitzpatrick skin type questionnaire [8]. Subsequently, a melanin and erythema measurement of normal skin was performed using narrow‐band reflectance spectrophotometry. The location of measurement of the normal skin corresponded with the location that was reflected in the Fitzpatrick questionnaire for ‘tanning habits’. In the burn and scar patient population, the location was usually on the contralateral or adjacent side of the scar on the unaffected part of the skin. Each measurement was performed twice and the averaged score was used for the analysis. Although an earlier study has shown that a single measurement obtains reliable results, multiple measurements are in general valuable to reduce measurement errors [20].

sec.title: Scar Database
sec.p: Scar quality assessment was performed between August 2004 and October 2022 in the outpatient clinic at the Burn Centre of the Red Cross Hospital in Beverwijk. All children and adults with burns or other traumatic wounds are followed up after their initial treatment via the outpatient aftercare and scar clinic. Patients visit this outpatient clinic for a routine follow‐up visit, including assessment of their scars, by a multidisciplinary aftercare team at 3 months post‐burn or post‐discharge. Thereafter, additional visits can be scheduled every 6–12 months when required. In the present study, scar quality assessments of > 1000 burn scar patients during the first year post‐burn, meaning at 3, 6 and 12 months post‐burn, were used.

During the evaluation, an assessment of the POSAS and narrow‐band reflectance spectrophotometry, measuring erythema and melanin of scar and uninjured skin, was performed. Measurement of melanin of uninjured skin was used to categorise burn scar patients in the different skin type categories as created using the skin database. Baseline characteristics such as sex, age at the time of the burn, percentage total burned surface area (TBSA), burn depth (partial or full thickness) and cause and anatomical location of the burn wound were collected. A partial thickness burn wound was defined as a wound that healed within 3 weeks upon conservative treatment and deep dermal/full thickness burn wounds were defined as requiring skin grafting.

For this study, the Medical Ethics Review Committee of the Amsterdam University Medical Centers ruled that the Medical Research Involving Human Subjects Act (WMO) was not applicable to this study (2022.068). Furthermore, the study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the Red Cross Hospital (22.024). Consent for the use of the routine care data in this study was implicit via the medical consult and based on the opt‐out protocol of the Red Cross Hospital, which is in accordance with national guidelines [35].

sec.title: Fitzpatrick Questionnaire
sec.p: The Fitzpatrick questionnaire consists of a five‐point scale with three topics concerning genetic disposition (colour of the eyes, hair and skin, and freckles), reaction to sun exposure and tanning habits. The maximum score of ‘genetic disposition’ and ‘sun exposure’ is 16 for each category; the maximum score of ‘tanning habits’ is eight, making the total score a maximum of 40 points. The sum score categorises an individual in one of the six Fitzpatrick skin type groups, varying from a lighter to a darker skin [8].

sec.title: Narrow‐Band Reflectance Spectrophotometry
sec.p: Objective measurements of normal skin were performed with the DermaSpectrometer (Cortex Technology, Hadsund, Denmark) till June 2012 and afterwards with its successor the DSM II ColorMeter (Cortex Technology, Hadsund, Denmark). Both objective colour measurement devices are narrow‐band reflectance spectrophotometers and are validated instruments to measure skin and scar colour [19, 20, 36, 37, 38]. DSM II uses a wide range of wavelengths (400–700 nm) for colour measurement, whereas the earlier model DermaSpectrometer uses 568 and 655 nm emitting diodes. They provide the parameters erythema (redness) and melanin index (pigmentation). During measurement, care was taken to avoid hairy areas and to prevent skin blanching after too much pressure as suggested by Clarys et al. [37].

sec.title: POSAS
sec.p: The POSAS is a reliable and validated scar assessment scale including different clinically important scar characteristics [27]. The items vascularization, pigmentation, thickness, surface roughness, pliability and overall opinion of the scar were scored by the observer (trained physician, researcher or research nurse). The patient independently scored the same scar for the items pain, pruritus, colour, pliability, thickness, relief and overall opinion. In the case of young children, the parents or caretakers completed the patient's scale. All the assessed items are scored on a 10‐point scale, with 1 representing normal skin and 10 representing the worst imaginable scar. Versions 1.0 and 2.0 were used; the surface area score of version 2.0 was not used in the analysis.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: To classify patients in different skin types, we created a categorical melanin score from the skin database. This was done by validating the melanin scores of unaffected skin, using narrow‐band reflectance spectrophotometry, against the score from the Fitzpatrick skin type questionnaire. Due to the low number of inclusions in categories I–II and V–VI, the skin type categories I + II and V + VI were combined in order to perform analysis. We first calculated the Spearman's rho correlation coefficient for melanin and total Fitzpatrick score, created a scatter plot for melanin versus total Fitzpatrick score (grouped by skin type), and performed Kruskal–Wallis 1‐way ANOVA to investigate differences in median melanin scores by skin type. Then, we created melanin cut‐off values for skin type categories by receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis and selected the values with the highest sensitivity and specificity. We performed this analysis separately for the DermaSpectrometer and the DSM II ColorMeter. This resulted in small differences in cut‐off values for which we decided to choose rounded values that fall between the results of the two versions of the device to have a simpler representation.

To investigate the association between the validated melanin device‐based skin type and burn scar formation, differences in scar quality for patients with different skin types were assessed using ANOVA. We analysed all data points within the first year post‐burn for each assessment moment separately, to assess potential trends in time. Scar quality comprised the following measures: the overall opinion POSAS score, the scores of the different POSAS items, and the mean score of the POSAS items, for both the observer and patient scales. Furthermore, scar quality in relation to skin type was assessed separately for children (< 18 years) and adults, and for burn cause (scalds versus other causes of burn).

All data was tested to determine that it was normally distributed. Statistical analysis was performed using IBM SPSS Statistics version 25 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, USA). A p‐value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

sec.title: Study Population and Characteristics
sec.p: A total of 137 healthy volunteers and patients were included in the skin database, and a total of 1039 patients with burn scars were included in the scar database. The mean age of the patients in the skin type and scar registration database was respectively 36.8 and 26.2 years (Table 1). In the skin database 42.3% were men; in the scar database 60.0% were men. The mean TBSA burned of patients in the scar database was 9.4% (SD 11.5).

sec.title: Skin Database: Relation Between Fitzpatrick Classification and Melanin Index
sec.p: There was a strong correlation (0.72 resp. 0.69 for Dermaspectrometer and DSM II) between melanin indices and the Fitzpatrick skin type classification using the sum scores of the questionnaire (Spearman's rho p < 0.0001). Also the correlation between the two instruments was highly significant: Spearman's rho 0.86, p < 0.001. Cutoff values were calculated using ROC curves and determining the highest specificity and sensitivity. Under 30.0 (Fitzpatrick Skin type I and II), between 30.0 and 34.9 (Skin type III), between 35.0 and 39.9 (Skin type IV) and 40.0 and above (Skin type V and VI) (Table 2).

caption.p: Cut off values skin type score for melanin.

fn.p: Note: Cutoff values between the skin type score and melanin score of the DermaSpectrometer and DSM II were analysed with a ROC curve to determine the highest sensitivity and 1‐specificity values.

sec.title: Scar Database: Skin Type and Scar Quality
sec.p: The 1039 burn patients in the scar database were scored at least at one of the three time points; 23% were scored only at 1 timepoint, 42% were scored at two time points and 35% were scored on all three time points. No DSM ColorMeter measurements were available for 30 individuals; these were not included in the analysis. Skin types were attributed based on the DSM ColorMeter measurements of uninjured skin. The distribution of patients over the different skin types is described in Table 1. Patient characteristics for individuals with different skin types are further specified in Table 3. From this, it can be noted that patient age, cause of burn and anatomical location were statistically different in the four skin type categories.

Scar outcomes at 3, 6 and 12 months post burn, based on DSM ColorMeter and POSAS measurements of the scars are described in Table 4. Higher erythema and vascularization were found for the earlier times post burn and in the lighter skin types. Pigmentation of the scars was assessed higher in the darker skin types and did not differ much over time. Other POSAS parameters were predominantly not statistically different between the different skin types, including parameters scored by patients.

caption.p: DSM colormeter and POSAS scores for different skin types at 3, 6 and 12 months post burn.

fn.p: Abbreviations: POSAS, patient and observer scar assessment scale; SD, standard deviation.
Erythema and melanin index: absolute value of scar minus value of uninjured skin.

sec.p: Since age and cause of burn differed among the patients with different skin types, we analysed the group of patients with scald burns versus other causes of burns. This did not reveal specific differences in scar outcome other than those reported for the whole group. Analysis of scar outcome in children versus adults did reveal some new insights. For most scar outcome parameters, children had less severe scar scores than adults (see Table S1). However, children had higher scores for scar thickness throughout the time course of scar maturation, and this was also true for children with lighter skin (Figures 1 and 2), despite the fact that their burns were smaller, less frequently surgically treated, and more frequently caused by hot fluids (Table S2). Children with Skin type I and II also scored their scars as significantly thicker than adults with Skin type I and II (Figure 2). Another striking observation was that adults gave higher pain scores than children throughout the whole period of scar maturation.

caption.p: Observer score for scar thickness in children and adults with different skin types during scar maturation. * indicates statistical significant differences of children versus adults (Anova).
Patient score for scar thickness in children and adults with different skin types during scar maturation. * indicates statistical significant differences of children versus adults (Anova).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our objectives were to investigate if skin type can be easily and reliably assessed by using a skin colour measurement device and whether skin type is of influence on scar quality in persons of different skin colour. Our results show that measurement of melanin indices can be used as an alternative to the Fitzpatrick questionnaire. Furthermore, in scars, vascularization and pigmentation were the main scar characteristics that were influenced by skin type.

In order to classify patients in skin types, we used the Fitzpatrick questionnaire. This questionnaire was originally developed for predicting reactivity to phototherapy, and later on it was also used to predict the skin's reaction to chemotherapy or to estimate the risk of skin cancer [5, 6]. A disadvantage of a self‐questionnaire is its subjective nature, making an objective measurement device preferable [39, 40]. In burn care, the Fitzpatrick skin type classification is relevant since evidence exists that patients with dark skin are more prone to scar formation [5]. Although it is believed that the classification from very light to dark skin is appropriate, assessment using the questionnaire is dubious. For instance, the item ‘tanning habits’ of the Fitzpatrick questionnaire has a relatively large contribution to the total score, but self‐reported sun burning and tanning aspects were shown to result in an unreliable means of skin typing [40]. Furthermore, only the ‘sun exposure’ subscale was found to have good internal consistency and construct validity, whereas the other two subscales ‘genetic disposition’ and ‘tanning habits’ did not [12].

Therefore, another method to determine skin type would be more appropriate. In our study we categorised skin type based on the melanin index measured by narrow‐band reflectance spectrophotometry. Melanin indices can be measured using different skin colour measurement devices. These devices have been validated using inter correlation coefficients and are deemed very feasible [19, 37, 41, 42].

In this study, skin type was determined by using the Fitzpatrick skin type classification questionnaire as well as the pigmentation parameter melanin index, as measured with an objective colour measurement device in 137 volunteers and patients. As expected, the Spearman's Rho correlation coefficient indicated a strong relation between the melanin score and the total score from the Fitzpatrick questionnaire. Nevertheless, the cutoff values we found differ somewhat from those reported by Sharma et al. [11], who made a similar analysis for an Indian population using a modified version of the Fitzpatrick questionnaire and different classifications for the skin type categories. They reported a cutoff of 42 between Skin type I–III versus IV and a cutoff of 47 for Skin type IV versus V and above. They did not report cutoff values for the lower skin type categories due to low numbers of observations in these categories. These results are in line with previous studies [3, 20, 43], including Van der Wal et al. who showed a validation between the DSM ColorMeter parameter melanin and the Fitzpatrick skin type classification, in a smaller sample size [20].

In the literature, dark skin is noted to be correlated with a higher incidence of keloids [5]. The relation with hypertrophic scarring is less explicitly demonstrated. In relation to scar prevention and treatment, it is relevant to know if certain skin types show a higher risk of developing more severe scarring. Therefore, we related the skin type as deducted from the measurement of the melanin index of uninjured skin to the scar outcome as registered in our scar database, comprising data of > 1000 patients.

The erythema measurement and POSAS vascularization item of the scars showed differences for the different skin types throughout the process of scar maturation, which was statistically significant for most of the time points. In general, the patients with lighter skin had higher vascularization/erythema scores for their scars, which declined after longer maturation times. This is in line with the fact that vascularization is the main determinant of skin colour for lighter skin [44] and with higher erythema during the inflammation phase of scar maturation [45].

Pigmentation scores of the POSAS were significantly higher for scars of patients with darker skin, indicating a greater difference from pigmentation compared to their normal skin. This may include hyper‐as well as hypopigmentation disorders. These scores did not decline very much during the scar maturation process, which indicates that pigmentation of the scars in these patients may remain aberrant from normal for a prolonged time. This is not unexpected, since pigmentation disorders are more likely to be noted in darker skin than in lighter skin.

Although hypertrophic scarring is also believed to be dependent on ethnicity [21] and thus skin types, we did not find an association between skin types and scar thickness. Also no significant differences were found for other scar parameters such as relief and pliability (Table 4).

In general, patient scores were higher overall than observer scores (Figures 1 and 2). This phenomenon was observed earlier [46, 47], and may be related to the fact that observers generally relate the severity of a scar characteristic to their experience in observing a wide range of scars while patients relate their judgment much more to their own specific scar. Furthermore, patient characteristics such as mental well‐being and coping mechanisms may be of influence [46].

As we found differences in patient characteristics across the skin types for age, cause of burn and anatomical location, we performed a sub‐analysis for patients with scalds versus other types of burns and for children under 5 years. These analyses gave similar results as for the whole group. However, the sub‐analysis of children under 18 years versus adults gave some new insights, especially for scar thickness. Throughout the time course of scar maturation, scar thickness was statistically significantly higher for children than for adults, initially only for patients with light skin, but at later time points also for the darker skin types. These data are in line with those described by Wallace et al., who found a consistent prevalence for raised scars in children, although these authors did not compare between children and adults [48].

Most other scar parameters were higher for adults than for children, albeit not statistically significant. In the group of children, there was a high representation of young children (< 5 years) with relatively light skin, with scald injuries, affecting the thorax (Table S2). These children suffered somewhat smaller burns, were less frequently operated on, and had a shorter LOS. Nevertheless, they had higher scores for scar thickness, which indicates a higher susceptibility for hypertrophic scar formation.

Adults expressed higher pain scores than children throughout the time course of follow‐up. This may reflect the fact that for small children, the opinion of the caregivers was asked as a proxy for the opinion of the young child. Our data suggest that the caregivers potentially underestimate the pain level experienced by the child. Therefore, other measures, such as the comfort scale, which is specifically designed to measure pain for young children, could be more suitable for this particular parameter [49]. Also, a specific POSAS scale for children is currently under development (F. Kemme, submitted).

The strength of our study includes the use of a large database of healthy individuals as well as burn patients. Nevertheless, our study also has some limitations. Despite the large database on scar measurements, loss to follow‐up can be noted at the later time points. This is likely due to patients not returning to the hospital if they experience no further complaints. In the scar database, skin colour measurements of the uninjured skin of burn patients were not on a fixed anatomical location, but usually on the contralateral side of a scar. This could have been a location on which there was a seasonal influence on skin colour and thus pigmentation. However, as burn patients are usually advised to avoid direct sun on their scar during the first 12 months after injury, it is likely that the uninjured contralateral side would also not have been exposed to extensive sun or tanning. Furthermore, differences in melanin and erythema scores related to anatomical sites have been described before [50], which were not accounted for in our data. Despite a reasonable sample size in the skin database, the extreme skin types, ie individuals with very pale and very dark skin types, were insufficiently present to be able to determine cutoff values using the DSM ColorMeter measurements. Furthermore, the specificity and selectivity still warrant improvement in further studies. Nevertheless, our data show that a good correlation exists between the Fitzpatrick questionnaire and the colour measurements, which could represent an easier and potentially more reliable determination of a person's skin type.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In this study, we used skin colour measurements as a means to determine a person's skin type. This measurement showed good correlation to the current standard Fitzpatrick classification but warrants further improvement for better discrimination. We showed that scar quality parameters—vascularization and pigmentation—are different between skin types. A lighter skin type was associated with higher erythema in scars. Furthermore, children developed thicker scars than adults, despite the fact that these burns were predominantly scald burns, smaller in size and required less surgical treatment than burns in adults. This was not only true for children with darker skin, but also, and even more pronounced, for children with light skin types. This indicates that a person's skin type influences burn scar development, and children are at higher risk of developing hypertrophic scars. Awareness of this risk helps physicians anticipate potential burn scar problems, take preventive measures and improve patient information regarding their burn scar development.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: For this study, the Medical Ethics Review Committee of the Amsterdam University Medical Centers ruled that the Medical Research Involving Human Subjects Act (WMO) was not applicable to this study (2022.068). Furthermore, the study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the Red Cross Hospital (22.024). Consent for the use of the routine care data in this study was implicit via the medical consult and based on the opt‐out protocol of the Red Cross Hospital, which is in accordance with national guidelines.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

caption.p: Table S1: DSM ColorMeter and POSAS scores for children and adults at 3, 6 and 12 months post burn.
Table S2:. Patient characteristics for children and adults.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors would like to thank the patients and volunteers for their participation in this study. Furthermore, we would like to thank David Wellenstein, Ella van Unen, Rozemarijn van Meijeren‐Hoogendoorn, Matthea Stoop, and Anouk van Heel for collecting part of the data.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author. The data are not publicly available due to privacy or ethical restrictions.

ref.mixed-citation: V.Gupta and V. K.Sharma, “Skin Typing: Fitzpatrick Grading and Others,” Clinics in Dermatology37, no. 5 (2019): 430–436, 10.1016/J.CLINDERMATOL.2019.07.010.31896400

V. K.Sharma, V.Gupta, B. O.Fasugba, A.Gardner, and W.Smyth, “The Fitzpatrick Skin Type Scale: A Reliability and Validity Study in Women Undergoing Radiation Therapy for Breast Cancer,” Journal of Wound Care23 (2014): 358–368.2504131210.12968/jowc.2014.23.7.358
